title: Dog Licked Antifreeze? Immediate Steps and Emergency Guide
meta_desc: If your dog licked antifreeze, it is a life-threatening emergency. Learn why immediate veterinary care is vital and what symptoms to watch for.
content_html:
TL;DR: Antifreeze ingestion is a life-threatening emergency for dogs that requires immediate veterinary intervention within 8 to 12 hours to prevent fatal kidney failure. If you suspect ingestion, contact an emergency vet or pet poison control immediately before symptoms appear.

What is antifreeze poisoning and why is it dangerous for my dog?

Antifreeze most commonly contains ethylene glycol, a chemical that is extremely toxic to dogs even in minute quantities. Because it has a sweet taste, dogs are often drawn to licking it off driveways or garage floors. Once ingested, the liver metabolizes the chemical into toxic byproducts that cause rapid, irreversible damage to the kidneys and central nervous system.

Is antifreeze ingestion considered a veterinary emergency?

  • This is a critical emergency with the highest level of urgency.
  • Ethylene glycol poisoning progresses through stages, and the window for effective treatment is extremely narrow—typically within 8 to 12 hours of ingestion.
  • Waiting for symptoms to appear often means the damage to the kidneys has already become fatal.
  • You must contact an emergency veterinarian or a pet poison control hotline immediately.

How does taking a photo of the antifreeze container help with my dog's triage?

  • If you can do so without delaying your trip to the vet, take a quick photo of the container or the spill.
  • This helps the medical team identify the active ingredients and concentration.
  • Some modern antifreeze brands use propylene glycol, which is less toxic than ethylene glycol, though still dangerous.
  • Knowing exactly what your dog ingested allows the vet to start the correct treatment protocol immediately upon your arrival.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Following ethylene glycol ingestion in dogs, immediate steps should include decontamination efforts; however, activated charcoal is unlikely to reduce absorption. Prompt veterinary intervention is crucial. Within 3 hours of ingestion, affected dogs may develop normochloremic metabolic acidosis with an increased anion gap and minimally concentrated urine with an acidic pH. Serum hyperosmolality with an increased osmolal gap may also be present. Monohydrate calcium oxalate crystals may be observed in urine as early as 6 hours post-ingestion. Ethylene glycol concentrations in serum and urine are detectable within 1-2 hours. Treatment focuses on preventing ethylene glycol metabolism using alcohol dehydrogenase inhibitors like 4-methylpyrazole (fomepizole) at a dose of 20 mg/kg IV initially, followed by 15 mg/kg IV at 12 and 24 hours, and 5 mg/kg IV at 36 hours. If 4-MP is unavailable, an ethanol regimen may be considered. Fluid therapy is essential to correct dehydration and increase urine production, facilitating ethylene glycol excretion. Hyperphosphatemia may also be seen in dogs within 3 hours of ingestion.

Chapter: Toxicology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 3048)

faqs.question: How much antifreeze is dangerous for a dog?
faqs.answer: Even a single lick or a teaspoon-sized amount can be lethal, especially for small to medium-sized dogs. There is no safe amount of ethylene glycol.

faqs.question: What are the early symptoms of ingestion?
faqs.answer: Initial symptoms often appear within 30 minutes and include 'drunken' behavior, wobbling, vomiting, excessive thirst, and lethargy.

faqs.question: Should I induce vomiting at home?
faqs.answer: Do not induce vomiting unless specifically instructed to do so by a licensed veterinarian or poison control expert, as it may not be safe depending on the dog's condition.
